ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken 65 or Hyderabadi Chicken 65; closest defensible match is the generic fried chicken food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Chicken 65 or Hyderabadi-style spiced fried chicken; A0CKM (chicken meat, fried) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Chicken 65 or Hyderabadi fried chicken as a distinct food concept.

allergens: gluten (cornstarch may contain trace gluten depending on brand)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Hyderabadi Chicken 65 is approximately 250 g (one plate), typically containing 6–8 pieces of bite-sized deep-fried spiced chicken.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken (fried / deep-fried preparation) adapted with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried (NDB 05062)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a dedicated entry for Hyderabadi Chicken 65. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried chicken data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05062 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried). Spice-blend and coating contributions (cornstarch, rice flour, chilli, turmeric, ginger-garlic paste) are estimated from USDA component data. Input record values (energy 260 kcal, protein 18 g, fat 18 g, carbohydrate 8 g, sodium 480 mg) are consistent with this adapted composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium is elevated due to salt, ginger-garlic paste, and spice blends typical of Hyderabadi preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283 — analogous estimate based on fried chicken with starch coating (GI ~45–50 range)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hyderabadi Chicken 65 in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated by analogy to fried chicken with a starch-based coating (cornstarch and rice flour), which falls in the low-GI range (GI 45–50) per Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. published tables. The high protein content (18 g/100 g) further attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (48 × 8.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 9.6 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg/100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g moderate threshold; a 250 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Hyderabadi Chicken 65 to a maximum portion of 100 g per occasion and request preparation with reduced salt and low-sodium spice blends to bring sodium below 400 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8 g/100 g) and high protein (18 g/100 g) attenuate postprandial glucose response; however, deep-frying adds significant fat (18 g/100 g) increasing caloric density and potentially promoting insulin resistance with frequent consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Hyderabadi Chicken 65 in portions of 100–120 g paired with non-starchy vegetables, avoiding rice or bread accompaniments, to maintain a low glycaemic load and manage total caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 18 g total fat and 4 g saturated fat per 100 g, with 0.2 g trans fat and 80 mg cholesterol; sodium at 480 mg/100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Hyderabadi Chicken 65 to occasional small portions (≤100 g) and prefer air-fried or tandoor-baked preparation to substantially reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and total caloric density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; high sodium (480 mg/100 g) exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations of Chicken 65 and request air-fried or tandoor-baked alternatives to eliminate AGE formation and reduce inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hyderabadi Chicken 65 is a popular spicy deep-fried chicken appetizer originating from Hyderabad, India, widely served across Indian restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of bite-sized boneless chicken pieces marinated in a bold blend of red chilli powder, turmeric, ginger-garlic paste, yogurt, and aromatic spices, then coated in a thin batter of cornstarch and rice flour before being deep-fried in hot oil until crisp and golden. The result is a dish with a fiery, tangy exterior, a juicy and tender interior, and a complex layering of heat, umami, and spice. Curry leaves and green chillies are typically added during or after frying to intensify the aroma. Despite being listed under Turkish or Kebab categories on some food delivery platforms, Chicken 65 is a distinctly South Indian dish with Hyderabadi roots. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 260 kcal, 18 g protein, 18 g fat (4 g saturated), 8 g carbohydrate, and 480 mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and moderates postprandial glucose response, giving it a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 48. However, the deep-frying method elevates total fat, saturated fat, trans fat, and caloric density, making it a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat load. It is suitable for halal diets. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 250 g (one plate), delivering around 650 kcal and 1200 mg sodium per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Hyderabadi Chicken 65?
Can I eat Chicken 65 if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No approved primary source contains a dedicated entry for Hyderabadi Chicken 65. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried chicken data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05062, fried chicken meat only), with spice and coating contributions estimated from USDA component data. Input record values are consistent with this adapted composite. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Hyderabadi Chicken 65 as a composite spiced fried dish are not available in ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. Cultural metadata corrected: input record incorrectly assigned Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma cuisine and Turkish ethnic tags; these have been replaced with Indian (Hyderabadi) cuisine and appropriate South Indian cultural context. Country of origin corrected from Singapore to India (Hyderabad). Duplicate portion entries in input (two entries both labelled is_default: true for 250 g) consolidated to a single 250 g default serving. Trans fat of 0.2 g/100 g is plausible for deep-fried preparations using partially hydrogenated oils common in commercial Indian restaurant cooking. Vitamin C at 4.0 mg/100 g is plausible from green chillies and curry leaves in the preparation.
